summary:
Oral tuberculosis usually presents with features such as central necrosis and granulomatous inflammation but rarely shows significant hyaline degeneration; thus, it can be distinguished from this by its absence. The lack of prominent hyaline change makes it a distinguishing feature not typical for these tuberculous lesions. \boxed{B}
The summary concisely captures that while many characteristics define oral tuberculosis, notable exclusion involves the rare occurrence of hyalinisation which differentiates it effectively when evaluating similar appearing lesions clinically. Thus, option B correctly identifies what should NOT accompany true TB-related mucosal findings.
